summary:
The French attempt to construct a canal across the Isthmus of Panama (1881–89) was one of the greatest engineering failures and financial scandals of the nineteenth century. Led by Ferdinand de Lesseps, the builder of the Suez Canal, the project mobilized vast French public investment, attracted enormous popular enthusiasm, and ended in catastrophic collapse — the deaths of approximately 22,000 workers from tropical diseases, the ruin of hundreds of thousands of small French investors, and a political scandal that shook the French Third Republic.

De Lesseps, riding the enormous prestige of Suez, promoted the Panama canal as a sea-level project analogous to Suez — ignoring engineers who warned that the mountainous terrain and the Chagres River, which flooded dramatically in the rainy season, made a sea-level canal far more difficult than anything at Suez. Work began in 1881 in terrible conditions: yellow fever and malaria killed workers at catastrophic rates, equipment was inadequate, and the engineering challenges proved intractable. By the time the original Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique went bankrupt in 1889, it had spent over 1.4 billion francs, excavated a fraction of the required material, and produced nothing functional.

The financial collapse triggered the Panama Affair, a political scandal of 1892–93 in which it emerged that the canal company had bribed hundreds of French legislators and journalists to suppress negative coverage and maintain investor confidence. The affair was a major political crisis, contributing to anti-Semitic conspiracy theories (the Jewish banker Jacques de Reinach was implicated and died during the investigation) and lasting damage to French republican politics. When the United States eventually built the canal between 1904 and 1914, it used a lock-based design with an artificial lake — the approach French engineers had advocated and de Lesseps had overruled.
